outlook.headline: Regionalization still helps, but breadth is fading
outlook.summary: Manufacturing footprint localization remains a clear competitive plus for companies tied to customer support, design wins, and supply-chain resiliency, with the strongest reads from Texas Instruments, GlobalFoundries, TE Connectivity, Amphenol, and Micron. But the topic’s mention trend has cooled to 0.3x and is running below trend, while several names are only talking about internal footprint tuning and Wolfspeed is flagging outright underutilization, so the next 1-2 quarters look more selective than broad-based.

drivers.label: Onshoring/regionalization supports design wins and engagements
drivers.detail: Customers are rewarding more localized manufacturing with incremental business and broader engagement activity.

drivers.label: Geopolitically dependable footprint improves customer support
drivers.detail: Proximity and regional resilience are helping companies serve customers faster and win sockets.

drivers.label: Capacity buildouts remain justified where supply is tight
drivers.detail: Expanding footprint and capacity is still constructive when demand is healthy and supply is constrained.

drivers.label: Flexible regional footprint helps navigate trade shifts
drivers.detail: A diversified manufacturing base is being used as a hedge against changing trade flows.

drivers.label: Underutilization is a clear exception
drivers.detail: Too much capacity relative to demand is pressuring the footprint at one supplier, showing the theme is not uniformly positive.

risks: Mention trend keeps sliding, suggesting the localization theme may be losing urgency even if still strategically important.
Capacity additions could ease tight supply faster than demand grows, reducing the near-term benefit for suppliers like Micron and Veeco.
Wolfspeed-style underutilization could broaden if demand fails to absorb existing regional capacity.
